label: Setup
title: Install ScavTemplate
lead: Create a template mod, point it at your local game install, and add CUCoreLib as a hard dependency.
bodyHtml:
Use ScavTemplate as the project shell

ScavTemplate gives you the BepInEx project shape so you are not starting from an empty C# library. CUCoreLib is added on top as a dependency.

  1. Open 05126619z/ScavTemplate.
  2. Create a new repository from the template or clone/download it.
    3. git clone https://github.com/05126619z/ScavTemplate
  3. Open the folder in whichever C# editor you prefer, or stay in the terminal and build with dotnet.

Template.sln works in Visual Studio or Rider, but it is not required. VS Code users can open the folder directly once the .NET SDK and C# tooling are installed.

Installing CUCoreLib

CUCoreLib is a library that provides a ton of useful functionality for BepInEx modding.

  1. Open jimmyking9999999/CUCoreLib/releases/.
  2. Download the latest CUCoreLib.dll and place it into your BepInEx\plugins folder.
    3. C:\Program Files (x86)\Steam\steamapps\common\Casualties Unknown Playtest\BepInEx\plugins
  3. Add the DLL as a reference in your mod project. The most universal way is to edit the .csproj file directly.
<ItemGroup>
  <Reference Include="CUCoreLib">
    <HintPath>..\..\BepInEx\plugins\CUCoreLib.dll</HintPath>
  </Reference>
</ItemGroup>

If your project lives somewhere else, change the HintPath so it points to the DLL you downloaded.

Editor shortcuts if you prefer the UI:

  • Visual Studio: right-click the project, choose Add > Reference, then browse to CUCoreLib.dll.
  • Rider: open the project file editor or project settings and add a local assembly reference.
  • VS Code: edit the .csproj directly. That is the normal path there.

Where the code goes

The code panel on the right shows the classic plugin shape CUCoreLib expects

In plugins.cs, copy-paste the right side code and change the namespace, class name, and BepInPlugin attributes to match your mod.

This line must be present for your mod to use CUCoreLib:

[BepInDependency("net.cucorelib", BepInDependency.DependencyFlags.HardDependency)]

Testing the mod

The ScavTemplate deploys your DLL when it builds.

  1. Build the project in Visual Studio with Build > Build Solution (Ctrl + Shift + B), or run dotnet build from the project folder via the terminal.
  2. Start the game. BepInEx will load your mod from BepInEx/plugins/<ProjectName>/<ProjectName>.dll.
dotnet build

If you are using an IDE, it is just calling the same build underneath. Visual Studio and Rider can both build the solution normally after the reference is added.

The compiled DLL also exists in your project output folder, bin/Debug/net48/<ProjectName>.dll.

If you are getting a 'skipping due to missing net.cucorelib' error, ensure that the CUCoreLib.dll is placed in the correct BepInEx\plugins folder and that the reference is properly added to your project.

Viewing BepInEx logs

Logs are how you confirm the mod loaded, spot missing references, and read your own Logger.LogInfo() messages. If the console or log file is disabled, you must enable it in the BepInEx config.

  1. Open BepInEx/config/BepInEx.cfg inside the game folder.
  2. Find [Logging.Console] and set Enabled = true to show the live console window.
  3. Find [Logging.Disk] and set Enabled = true to write BepInEx/LogOutput.log.
  4. Launch the game and look for your plugin's startup line, such as Plugin ModName is loaded!.
[Logging.Console]

## Enables showing a console for log output.
# Setting type: Boolean
# Default value: false
Enabled = true

[Logging.Disk]

## Include unity log messages in log file output.
# Setting type: Boolean
# Default value: false
WriteUnityLog = false

Why is my Start()/Update() not working?

Casualties: Unknown may have destroyed your plugin's game object. There is a BepInEx configuration option that can fix this

BepInEx -> Config -> bepinex.cfg -> HideManagerGameObject = true

[Chainloader]
      ## If enabled, hides BepInEx Manager GameObject from Unity.
      # Setting type: Boolean
      # Default value: false
      HideManagerGameObject = true

CUCoreLib using statements

After referencing CUCoreLib, you will need some namespaces imported at the top of your plugin files. You do not need every namespace in every file. Rather, these just make CUCoreLib classes available without typing their full namespace. Very neat, very handy.

The supported public API for downstream mods is the documented surface in CUCoreLib.Data, CUCoreLib.Registries, CUCoreLib.Saving, and the intentionally public helpers documented here such as AssetLoader, CUCoreUtils, and CustomInstantiate. Types outside that documented surface are implementation details and may change without compatibility guarantees.

using CUCoreLib.Helpers;
using CUCoreLib.Registries;
using CUCoreLib.Data;
using CUCoreLib.Saving;
Namespace Use it for Common early examples
CUCoreLib.HelpersSelected public helper APIs.AssetLoader, CUCoreUtils, CustomInstantiate.
CUCoreLib.RegistriesRegistering content or commands.ItemRegistry, RecipeRegistry, LocaleRegistry, ConsoleCommandRegistry.
CUCoreLib.DataExtra data types for advanced registration.CustomItemInfo, container properties, battery properties, custom recipe data.
CUCoreLib.SavingSave provider interfaces and save registration.ICustomSaveProvider, SaveRegistry.

For most beginner item/recipe mods, Helpers and Registries are the important two. Avoid depending on patch classes or undocumented helper internals, because those are free to change as CUCoreLib evolves.
